message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it from the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Find the code path/contract/test that makes the PoC impossible or moot. Only survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

E: numpy resample path claimed "exactly equal" / "never a semantic change" — diverges on NaN in null
File: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:224
Description: _resample_stability documents that the numpy path "computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts ... so its result is exactly equal to the Python path; it is purely a speed optimization ... never a semantic change." This holds for finite values but not for NaN. The pure-Python path uses `v >= observed` / `v <= observed`, where any NaN comparison is False (NaN never counts as extreme). The numpy path uses np.sort + np.searchsorted on a float64 array; numpy sorts NaN to the end and searchsorted against that ordering yields different extreme counts than the Python comparison count. With a NaN present in `null` the two paths can produce different p-values and therefore different stability fractions — a semantic divergence the docstring rules out.
PoC: from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import _resample_stability
null = [0.0, 1.0, float('nan'), 2.0]
resamples = [1.5]
# Python path: counts only finite v >= 1.5 -> extreme=1 ; numpy path: NaN sorted last shifts searchsorted index -> different extreme count, so the two paths can disagree on (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha.

content.content:
160	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
161	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
162	
163	    Direction (pass 4, #G-017): unlike :func:`confirm_whole_corpus` — where the verdict polarity
164	    is derived from the pre-registered ``predicted_direction`` tail — the unit-level verdict is
165	    governed SOLELY by the caller-supplied ``direction_supports_claim`` flag (the engine sees only
166	    an opaque held-out ``statistic``/``p_value`` and cannot itself infer the tail). The
167	    hypothesis's ``predicted_direction`` is therefore advisory at unit level: it is shape-validated
168	    below for safety but does NOT decide the verdict. A study that wants the locked tail to bind the
169	    unit-level decision must derive ``direction_supports_claim`` from it in its own confirmer.
170	    """
171	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
172	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
173	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
174	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
175	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
176	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
177	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
178	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
179	    # against an unrecognized tail.
180	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
181	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
182	    ):
183	        raise ValueError(
184	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
185	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
186	        )
187	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
188	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
189	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
190	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
191	        statistic=statistic,
192	        p_value=p_value,
193	        alpha=alpha,
194	        powered=powered,
195	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
196	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
197	    )
198	
199	
200	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
201	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
202	    n = len(null)
203	    if tail == "greater":
204	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
205	    else:
206	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
207	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
208	
209	
210	def _resample_stability(
211	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
212	) -> float:
213	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
214	
215	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
216	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
217	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
218	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
219	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
220	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
221	
222	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
223	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
224	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
225	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
226	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
227	    """
228	    n = len(null)
229	    try:
230	        import numpy as np
231	
232	        # Use searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) rather than the
233	        # R×N broadcast boolean matrix (#G-010): the matrix peaks at ~R*N bytes (250MB for
234	        # R=5000,N=50000) and can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not. The integer
235	        # extreme-counts are IDENTICAL to _empirical_p: for 'greater', count(null >= r) = N -
236	        # searchsorted(sorted, r, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= r) = searchsorted(sorted, r,
237	        # 'right'). Then (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha, same as the pure path.
238	        sorted_null = np.sort(np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64))
239	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
240	        if tail == "greater":
241	            extreme = n - np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="left")
242	        else:
243	            extreme = np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="right")
244	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
245	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
246	    except ImportError:
247	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
248	        return significant / len(resamples)
249	
250	
251	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
252	
253	
254	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
255	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time argument is
256	    optional and, if given, must MATCH the pre-registered one — it can cross-check, never supply.
257	
258	    A hypothesis reaching here has already passed the confirm gate, so it is locked and its
259	    ``predicted_direction`` is part of the cryptographic binding. A ``None`` locked direction
260	    therefore means the tail was *not* pre-registered; accepting a confirm-time direction in that
261	    case would let the analyst pick the tail AFTER seeing the data (HARKing), flipping
262	    supported↔contradicted on a hypothesis whose lock attests "no direction" (audit pass 4,
263	    #G-001). The tail must be committed before confirmation, so a directionless locked hypothesis
264	    is refused outright rather than completed with a post-hoc direction.
265	
266	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}``; any other value is
267	    rejected (an unrecognized tail would silently fall through to 'less' in :func:`_empirical_p`,
268	    flipping the verdict — audit pass 2, #128).
269	    """
270	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
271	    if locked_dir is None:
272	        raise ValueError(
273	            "predicted_direction was not pre-registered: a whole-corpus confirmatory hypothesis "
274	            "must lock its tail ('greater' or 'less') BEFORE confirmation. Supplying a confirm-time "
275	            "direction for a directionless locked hypothesis selects the tail after seeing the data "
276	            "(HARKing) and is refused (#G-001)."
277	        )
278	    if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
279	        raise ValueError(
280	            "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
281	            f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
282	        )
283	    resolved = locked_dir
284	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
285	        raise ValueError(
286	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
287	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
288	        )
289	    return resolved
290	
291	
292	def confirm_whole_corpus(
293	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
294	    *,
295	    observed: float,
296	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
297	    alpha: float,
298	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
299	    stability_threshold: float = 0.9,

content.text: The finding's core claim is about NaN behavior. Let me verify the numpy semantics empirically and check whether NaN can actually reach this code.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "
import numpy as np
null = [0.0, 1.0, float('nan'), 2.0]
resamples = [1.5]
tail='greater'; alpha=0.5
n=len(null)

# python path
def emp(null, observed, tail):
    n=len(null)
    if tail=='greater':
        ex=sum(1 for v in null if v>=observed)
    else:
        ex=sum(1 for v in null if v<=observed)
    return (ex+1)/(n+1)
py = sum(1 for r in resamples if emp(null,r,tail)<=alpha)/len(resamples)

# numpy path
sorted_null=np.sort(np.asarray(null,dtype=np.float64))
print('sorted_null', sorted_null)
res=np.asarray(resamples,dtype=np.float64)
extreme = n - np.searchsorted(sorted_null,res,side='left')
p=(extreme+1)/(n+1)
npp=float(int((p<=alpha).sum())/len(resamples))
print('py emp p', emp(null,1.5,tail), 'py stab', py)
print('np extreme', extreme, 'np p', p, 'np stab', npp)
print('DIVERGE', py!=npp)
"

import numpy as np
null = [0.0, 1.0, float('nan'), 2.0]; resamples=[1.5]; tail='greater'; alpha=0.5; n=4
def emp(null,observed,tail):
    n=len(null); ex=sum(1 for v in null if v>=observed) if tail=='greater' else sum(1 for v in null if v<=observed)
    return (ex+1)/(n+1)
py=sum(1 for r in resamples if emp(null,r,tail)<=alpha)/len(resamples)
sn=np.sort(np.asarray(null,dtype=np.float64)); print('sorted',sn)
res=np.asarray(resamples,dtype=np.float64)
ex=n-np.searchsorted(sn,res,side='left'); p=(ex+1)/(n+1)
npp=float(int((p<=alpha).sum())/len(resamples))
print('py p',emp(null,1.5,tail),'pyStab',py,'npExtreme',ex,'npP',p,'npStab',npp,'DIVERGE',py!=npp)
"

content.text:
The mechanism is confirmed: with a NaN in `null`, the two paths diverge (Python stability 1.0 vs numpy 0.0). numpy sorts NaN to the end, so searchsorted counts it as "greater than 1.5", inflating the extreme count.

Now the critical question: can a NaN actually reach `_resample_stability`? Let me check the call path and whether there's input validation upstream.

content.text:
This is the decisive point. The only caller of `_resample_stability` is `confirm_whole_corpus`, and before the call:

- Line 329-330: every value in `null_distribution` is `_validate_finite`-checked → raises `ValueError` on NaN.
- Line 345-346: every `resample_statistics` value is `_validate_finite`-checked → raises on NaN.

`_validate_finite` uses `math.isfinite`, which is False for NaN. So a NaN in `null` can never reach `_resample_stability` through the public path — the function raises before line 347.

Let me confirm `_resample_stability` is not called/exported anywhere else.

input.description: Find all references to the helpers

content.text:
`_resample_stability` is a private module-level function (leading underscore) and its only production caller is `confirm_whole_corpus`, which finite-validates both `null_distribution` and `resample_statistics` before invoking it. The PoC calls the private function directly with raw lists, bypassing the engine's contract.

Let me assess the finding's claim precisely. The finding asserts a "semantic divergence the docstring rules out." Let me re-read what the docstring actually claims (lines 224-226): the numpy path "is exactly equal to the Python path... never a semantic change."

This is a docstring-scope question. The finding is technically correct that:
1. The two paths genuinely diverge on NaN (empirically confirmed: 1.0 vs 0.0).
2. The docstring's "exactly equal / never a semantic change" is stated unconditionally.

But it's only reachable by calling the private helper directly. The finding's PoC does exactly that. The question for survival is whether the docstring claim is falsifiable within the function's actual contract. The docstring describes the function's own behavior ("its result is exactly equal to the Python path"), and that statement is provably false for a NaN-containing input the function will accept and process without error. The function itself has no NaN precondition documented or enforced. So as a statement about `_resample_stability` in isolation, the docstring overclaims.

However, the finding is framed strongly: it claims this is a real semantic divergence in p-values/stability fractions. In the actual system, it cannot happen — every public entry point rejects NaN at lines 330 and 346 before `_resample_stability` runs. So the PoC's claimed consequence ("different p-values and therefore different stability fractions") is unreachable in production; it's a docstring-accuracy issue, not a behavioral bug.

Let me weigh: the refuter standard is "survives=true only if I genuinely cannot disprove it." I can disprove the *behavioral* claim (divergence in real use) via the finite guards. But the finding's literal subject is the docstring's "exactly equal... never a semantic change" claim, and I confirmed that claim is false for an input the function accepts. The docstring is part of the code and it does overclaim.

The finding explicitly says the divergence makes "different p-values and therefore different stability fractions — a semantic divergence the docstring rules out." That outcome requires NaN to reach the function, which the guards prevent. The core asserted impact is moot. The remaining true kernel is merely "the docstring's universal phrasing is technically imprecise for an unvalidated direct call" — a documentation nuance, not the bug as described.

input.reasoning:
The NaN divergence mechanism is real — I reproduced it empirically (in a venv with numpy): for null=[0.0,1.0,nan,2.0], resamples=[1.5], tail='greater', alpha=0.5 the pure-Python path returns stability 1.0 while the numpy searchsorted path returns 0.0, because np.sort puts NaN last and searchsorted then counts it toward the 'greater' extreme. So the docstring's literal "exactly equal / never a semantic change" is technically imprecise IF a NaN reaches _resample_stability.

But the finding's stated impact — "different p-values and therefore different stability fractions" in actual use — is unreachable, which moots it:

1. _resample_stability is a private helper (leading underscore). Its ONLY production caller is confirm_whole_corpus (confirm.py:347,351). The PoC invokes the private function directly with raw lists, bypassing the engine contract.

2. confirm_whole_corpus finite-validates every input before the call: null_distribution at lines 329-330 (`for v in null_distribution: _validate_finite(...)`) and resample_statistics at lines 345-346. _validate_finite (line 96-98) uses math.isfinite, which is False for NaN, so it raises ValueError on any NaN. A NaN in `null` therefore raises at line 330 long before _resample_stability runs at line 347 — the divergent path is never entered.

3. grep confirms no other production caller of _resample_stability; the only other reference is a test.

The divergence cannot produce divergent p-values/stability fractions through any public entry point because both numpy and pure paths are only reached after NaN has been rejected. The finding's asserted behavioral consequence is impossible in the real system; what remains is at most an over-broad docstring phrasing for a direct private-call scenario the engine's contract excludes. Disproved.

content.text:
I've completed the adversarial refutation.

Finding E does not survive. The NaN divergence between the numpy and Python paths is genuinely real (I reproduced it: 1.0 vs 0.0 stability), so the docstring's universal "exactly equal / never a semantic change" phrasing is technically imprecise. But the finding's asserted impact — divergent p-values/stability fractions in actual use — is unreachable: `_resample_stability` is a private helper whose only production caller, `confirm_whole_corpus`, finite-validates both `null_distribution` (confirm.py:329-330) and `resample_statistics` (lines 345-346) via `math.isfinite` before invoking it. Any NaN raises `ValueError` before the divergent path runs. The PoC only triggers divergence by calling the private function directly, bypassing the engine contract.

Relevant file: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py` (guards at lines 96-98, 329-330, 345-346; helper at 210-248; sole caller at 347/351).
